points: Biodiversity is a complex ecological web where humans and all organisms depend on each other for survival; forests act as primary producers supporting this entire system.
The Indian Wildlife (Protection) Act of 1972 provided the legal framework for habitat protection and banning of hunting to save endangered species across the country.
Launched in 1973, Project Tiger is a major conservation effort that focuses on preserving the dwindling tiger population and their biotypes across various national reserves like Corbett and Manas.
Forests in India are managed by the government and classified into three types: Reserved (most valuable), Protected (safe from depletion), and Unclassed (govt/community owned).
Reserved and Protected forests together form permanent forest estates, with Madhya Pradesh holding the largest area (75%) under this category for timber and protection.
While some states focus on reserved or protected forests, North-eastern states and parts of Gujarat have a high percentage of unclassed forests managed by local communities.
Local communities have long played a role in conservation, successfully resisting mining in Sariska and deforestation in the Himalayas through the famous Chipko movement.
Sacred Groves are pristine forest patches preserved by tribal societies (like Mundas and Santhals) based on nature worship, where interference with trees and wildlife is banned.
Joint Forest Management (JFM) is a collaborative approach documented first in Odisha in 1988, involving local village institutions in the restoration of degraded forest lands.
